question_text: a) Predict the products of the reaction CC(=O)CCOC1CCCCO1 + Ph3P-CH3^(+) Br^(-) = {box_a} + {box_b} b) Decrribe a mechanism to account for the formation of compounds A and B.
canonical_answer: a) {box_a}:= C=CCCCCO; {box_b}:= C=CC(C)=O; b) The reaction sequence begins with the addition of sodium hydride, which acts as a base to deprotonate the starting material at the carbon position alpha to the ether linkage and beta to the carbonyl group. This deprotonation triggers an elimination reaction that breaks the carbon-oxygen bond of the tetrahydropyranyl group, resulting in the formation of methyl vinyl ketone, and a cyclic alkoxide intermediate. This alkoxide then undergoes a spontaneous ring-opening rearrangement where the negative oxygen atom forms a new carbonyl group, breaking the internal carbon-oxygen bond of the ring to produce 5-hydroxypentanal in its alkoxide form. This alkoxide species then acts as a base to deprotonate the methyltriphenylphosphonium cation, which generates a reactive Wittig ylide and converts the alkoxide into a terminal alcohol group. The nucleophilic carbon of the Wittig ylide attacks the electrophilic carbonyl of the aldehyde, leading to the formation of a four-membered oxaphosphetane ring intermediate. Finally, the oxaphosphetane collapses to release triphenylphosphine oxide and generate a terminal carbon-carbon double bond, yielding the final product 5-hexen-1-ol.

question_text: Compound {box_a} has the molecular formula C8H17N. Treatment of {box_a} with excess methyl iodide, followed by silver oxide and heating, gives the pure (S)-enantiomer of N,N-dimethyolt-7-ene-3-amine. a) Propose a stereochemically accurate structure for compound {box_a}; b) Describe a mechanism to show how this reaction gives observed product.
canonical_answer: a) {box_c}:= CC[C@H]1CCC[C@@H](C)N1; b) The process begins with the exhaustive methylation of Compound C using excess methyl iodide to convert the secondary amine into a quaternary ammonium iodide salt. The addition of silver oxide and water then replaces the iodide counterion with hydroxide, which serves as the necessary base for the Hofmann elimination. For the elimination to proceed via an E2 mechanism, the piperidine ring must adopt a chair conformation in which the bulky quaternary ammonium leaving group is positioned axially, allowing it to be anti-periplanar to a beta-hydrogen atom. Regioselectivity is governed by Hofmann's rule, where the hydroxide base preferentially abstracts a hydrogen from the least substituted available beta-carbon—specifically the C6 methylene group—because these hydrogens are sterically more accessible than those on more substituted carbons. As the base removes the anti-periplanar beta-proton, the carbon-hydrogen bond breaks, a new carbon-carbon double bond forms, and the carbon-nitrogen bond cleaves, causing the ring to open and form a terminal alkene. The stereocenter originally at the C2 position, which carries the ethyl group, remains intact during this process. Because the starting material possesses specific stereochemistry, the ring-opening and subsequent priority changes of the substituents around the ethyl-bearing carbon result in the formation of the pure (S)-enantiomer of N,N-dimethyloct-7-en-3-amine. This particular diastereomer of Compound C is the required starting material because it provides the necessary anti-periplanar geometry for the elimination while leading to the specific (S) configuration in the final acyclic product.

question_text: Describe a reaction coordinate diagram for a reaction with the following criteria: a) exergonic, 3-step reaction b) the first-step is the rate-determining step c) the second intermediate is more stable than the first intermediate d) the third step of the reaction is faster than the reverse of the second step
canonical_answer: The reaction coordinate diagram plots energy on the vertical axis against the progress of the reaction on the horizontal axis. The curve begins with the reactants at a specific energy level. To represent a three-step reaction, the diagram features three distinct transition state peaks separated by two intermediate valleys. Since the first step is the rate-determining step, the first transition state peak is the highest point on the entire graph, representing the largest activation energy barrier. After this first peak, the curve drops into the first intermediate valley. The energy then rises to a second transition state peak, which remains lower than the first, and descends into a second intermediate valley. This second intermediate valley is positioned at a lower energy level than the first intermediate to indicate it is more stable. To show that the third step is faster than the reverse of the second step, the energy barrier rising from the second intermediate to the third transition state peak is smaller than the barrier rising from the second intermediate back to the second transition state peak. Finally, the curve descends from the third transition state to the products. To be exergonic, the final products are placed at a lower energy level than the initial reactants, resulting in a negative change in Gibbs free energy for the overall process.

question_text: Propose a mechanism for the following reaction. Ignore stereochemistry. C=CC1CC1C + Br2 = CC1CCC1(Br)CBr
canonical_answer: The reaction begins with an electrophilic addition where the pi-bond of the vinyl group attacks a bromine molecule, displacing a bromide ion and forming a cyclic bromonium ion intermediate across the two alkenyl carbons. This intermediate then undergoes a spontaneous ring expansion driven by the relief of ring strain. One of the carbon-carbon bonds within the three-membered cyclopropane ring breaks and shifts to attack the carbon of the bromonium ion that is directly attached to the ring. This migration simultaneously opens the three-membered bromonium ring and expands the original cyclopropane into a four-membered cyclobutane ring. The resulting intermediate is a cyclobutane derivative with a bromomethyl group and a secondary carbocation located on the ring at the position from which the carbon-carbon bond migrated. In the final step, the previously displaced bromide ion acts as a nucleophile and attacks the carbocation, yielding the final vicinal dibromide product.

question_text: Provide mechanisms for the following reactions. Ignore stereochemistry. a) BR3 + H2O2, NaOH = 3 ROH + B(OH)3; b) CC1=C(C)CCCC1 + O3 = CC12CCCCC(C)(OO1)O2
canonical_answer: a) The oxidative workup of a trialkylborane, begins with the deprotonation of hydrogen peroxide by hydroxide base to generate the nucleophilic hydroperoxide anion. This anion attacks the electrophilic boron atom, forming a negatively charged boronate complex. An alkyl group then migrates from the boron to the adjacent oxygen atom, which simultaneously breaks the weak oxygen-oxygen bond and expels a hydroxide ion, converting one carbon-boron bond into a boron-oxygen-alkyl linkage. This sequence of nucleophilic attack and alkyl migration repeats for the second and third alkyl groups until a trialkyl borate is formed. Subsequently, the borate undergoes base-catalyzed hydrolysis where hydroxide ions attack the boron center to displace the three alkoxide groups. Finally, these released alkoxide ions are protonated to yield three equivalents of the alcohol product and boric acid; b) The formation of an ozonide from an alkene, starts with a concerted [3+2] cycloaddition between the ozone molecule and the carbon-carbon double bond. This step creates a highly unstable five-membered ring intermediate known as a molozonide, which contains three consecutive oxygen atoms. The molozonide then undergoes a spontaneous retro-cycloaddition fragmentation, breaking the ring into two distinct fragments: a carbonyl compound and a dipolar carbonyl oxide species. These two fragments realign and undergo a second [3+2] cycloaddition, where the carbonyl oxide adds back across the carbonyl group. This final ring-closing step produces the more stable secondary ozonide, a five-membered ring containing a peroxide bridge and an ether oxygen.

question_text: Provide the major elimination product and a detailed description of the mechanism for the following reaction. Specify correct stereochemistry where appropiate. C[C@@H]1CCCC[C@H]1I + t-BuOK, t-BuOH = {box_1}.
canonical_answer: {box_1}:= C[C@H]1C=CCCC1; The starting material is trans-1-iodo-2-methylcyclohexane, which undergoes an E2 elimination upon treatment with the strong, bulky base potassium tert-butoxide. The E2 mechanism specifically requires the leaving group and the beta-hydrogen to be in an anti-periplanar orientation, which in a cyclohexane ring means both must occupy axial positions. When the iodine atom is placed in an axial position to allow for elimination, the adjacent methyl group at the C2 position is equatorial. This leaves only the hydrogen atom at the C6 position in an axial, anti-periplanar relationship to the iodine. The hydrogen at C2 is on the same face of the ring as the axial iodine and therefore cannot satisfy the geometric requirement for elimination. Consequently, the base selectively abstracts the axial hydrogen from C6, leading to the formation of 3-methylcyclohexene as the major product. This outcome overrides Zaitsev's rule, as the more substituted 1-methylcyclohexene cannot be formed due to these strict stereoelectronic constraints.

question_text: Provide the major elimination product and a detailed description of the mechanism for the following reaction. Specify correct stereochemistry where appropiate. CC[C@@H](Br)[C@@H](C)C(C)C + EtO^(-)/EtOH = {box_1}.
canonical_answer: {box_1}:= CC/C=C(C)\C(C)C; The secondary alkyl bromide reacts with the small, strong base sodium ethoxide via an E2 mechanism. Unlike cyclic systems, acyclic molecules can rotate freely around single bonds to achieve the necessary anti-periplanar geometry between a beta-hydrogen and the bromine leaving group. Because ethoxide is a relatively small base, the regiochemistry of the reaction is governed by Zaitsev's rule, which favors the formation of the most stable, most highly substituted alkene. The base preferentially abstracts a hydrogen from the more substituted beta-carbon. During this concerted process, the molecule adopts a staggered conformation where the chosen hydrogen and the bromine are anti-periplanar to minimize electronic repulsion. The resulting major product is the more substituted alkene, specifically the (E)-diastereomer, because this configuration minimizes steric clashing between the bulky alkyl groups during the transition state, making it lower in energy than the corresponding (Z)-isomer.

question_text: Describe a mechanism that accounts for each of the following products a)-c) of the reaction CC(C)C(C)Br + EtOH, heating: a) Product -  CCOC(C)C(C)C; b) Product - CCOC(C)CC; c) Product - C/C=C(C)/C.
canonical_answer: a) An ethanol molecule acts as a nucleophile and attacks the secondary carbocation. This is followed by a deprotonation step where another ethanol molecule removes the proton from the resulting oxonium ion to yield the neutral ether; b) Because carbocations are prone to rearrangement, the secondary carbocation can undergo a 1,2-hydride shift from the adjacent tertiary carbon. This shift creates a more stable tertiary carbocation. To form the second substitution product, 2-ethoxy-2-methylbutane, an ethanol molecule attacks this rearranged tertiary carbocation. Subsequent deprotonation of the intermediate oxonium ion by the solvent produces the tertiary ether; c) To form the elimination product, 2-methyl-2-butene, an ethanol molecule acts as a base and abstracts a beta-proton from a carbocation intermediate. While protons can be removed from several positions, the major E1 product is determined by Zaitsev’s rule, which favors the formation of the most stable, most highly substituted alkene. By abstracting a proton from the tertiary carbon adjacent to the positive charge in the secondary carbocation (or an equivalent proton in the tertiary carbocation), a trisubstituted double bond is formed, resulting in the observed major alkene product.

question_text: Predict the dipole moment of calicene c2ccc(=c1cc1)c2, if there is one. Hint: think about aromaticity.
canonical_answer: Calicene possesses a significant permanent dipole moment that points from the three-membered ring toward the five-membered ring. This electronic distribution occurs because the molecule is best described by a dipolar resonance form where the central double bond is polarized. In this resonance contributor, the electrons shift toward the five-membered ring, leaving the three-membered ring with a positive charge and the five-membered ring with a negative charge. This charge separation is exceptionally stable because it allows both rings to become aromatic according to Hückel's rule: the three-membered ring becomes the cyclopropenyl cation with 2 pi-electrons, and the five-membered ring becomes the cyclopentadienyl anion with 6 pi-electrons. Due to this gain in aromatic stability, there is a strong shift in electron density toward the five-membered ring, resulting in a dipole moment with its positive end on the smaller ring and its negative end on the larger ring.

question_text: a) Choose which molecule is more stable: 1) O=c1cccccc1, or 2) O=c1cccc1?  b) Explain why in 1-2 sentences.
canonical_answer: a) 1; b) Tropone, the seven-membered ring, is more stable because the polarization of its carbonyl group creates a dipolar resonance structure featuring a highly stable aromatic six-pi-electron ring. In contrast, the corresponding resonance contributor for cyclopentadienone is highly unstable because it contains an antiaromatic four-pi-electron system.

question_text: Propose a mechanism for the following transformation: O=C1CCCC1 + C1COCCN1 + Cc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)O)cc1 = C2=C(N1CCOCC1)CCC2. Hint:  Cc1ccc(S(=O)(=O)O)cc1 is an acid.
canonical_answer: First, the carbonyl oxygen of cyclopentanone is protonated by p-toluenesulfonic acid. This generates an oxonium ion, making the carbonyl carbon electrophilic. Next, the secondary amino group pf morpholine acts as a nucleophile and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon. This addition step breaks the pi bond of the carbonyl group and forms a tetrahedral intermediate containing a positively charged ammonium group and a hydroxyl group. A proton transfer process then occurs. The nitrogen atom loses a proton, while the hydroxyl oxygen gains a proton. The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom pushes down to form a carbon-nitrogen double bond, expelling the molecule of water. This results in the formation of a positively charged iminium ion intermediate. Finally, a p-toluenesulfonate anion abstracts an acidic proton from the alpha-carbon adjacent to the C=N bond. The electrons from this C−H  bond shift to form a carbon-carbon double bond, neutralizing the positive charge on the nitrogen and yielding the final neutral enamine product.

question_text: Propose a detailed mechanism for the reaction below: c1ccccc1+ C1=CCCCC1 + HF = c2ccc(C1CCCCC1)cc2
canonical_answer: First, cyclohexene is protonated by hydrofluoric acid. The pi electrons of the cyclohexene double bond attack the proton of HF, forming a secondary cyclohexyl carbocation and a fluoride ion. This cyclohexyl carbocation is a potent electrophile. Then, the aromatic pi system of benzene acts as a nucleophile and attacks the electrophilic cyclohexyl carbocation. This is the rate-determining step (RDS) and leads to the formation of a resonance-stabilized carbocation intermediate. Finally, to restore the ring's aromaticity, the fluoride ion acts as a base and abstracts the proton from the sp3-hybridized carbon of the arenium ion—the same carbon to which the cyclohexyl group is attached. The electrons from the carbon-hydrogen bond return to the ring, re-establishing the aromatic system and forming the final product, cyclohexylbenzene.

(d) A one-step reaction where the conversion to reactants is faster than conversion to products. Is ΔG positive or negative?
canonical_answer: (a) Energy is plotted on the vertical axis, reaction progress on the horizontal axis. The graph starts with a plateau (reactant), passes over a first barrier, and falls into a valley (intermediate), which is higher in energy than the starting plateau. Then, a transition occurs over a second barrier to a final plateau (product). The product plateau is higher in energy than the reactant plateau, but lower than the intermediate valley. The energy of the first barrier is higher than the energy of the second barrier.; (b) Energy is plotted on the vertical axis, reaction progress on the horizontal axis. The graph starts with a plateau (reactant), passes over a first barrier, and falls into a valley (intermediate), which is higher in energy than the starting plateau. Then it crosses a second barrier, which is the highest peak, and falls into a second valley (intermediate), which is higher in energy than the first intermediate valley. Finally, it passes over a third barrier, which is higher in energy than first barrier, but lower in energy than second barrier, and drops to a final plateau (product), which is higher in energy than the starting reactant plateau, but lower than the first intermediate valley.; (c) Energy is plotted on the vertical axis, reaction progress on the horizontal axis. The graph starts with a plateau (reactant), passes over a first barrier, and falls into a valley (intermediate). Energy of first intermediate is higher, than the reactant energy. Then it rises to a second barrier, which is the highest point on the graph, and falls into a second valley, which is lower in energy than first valley, but higher in energy than the reactant plateau. Then, a transition occurs over a third barrier, which is the lowest of the three, to a final plateau (product). The product plateau is lower in energy than the starting reactant plateau. The third barrier is lower in energy than first intermediate.; (d) Energy is plotted on the vertical axis, reaction progress on the horizontal axis. question_text: Provide the mechanisms and products for the following reactions. Provide distinct structures for all intermediates. First reaction is C=CCCCCN + Br2, second reaction is C=C(C)C(C)c1ccccc1 + HBr.
canonical_answer: The first reaction presents the intramolecular cyclization of an amino-alkene in the presence of bromine. The mechanism initiates with the formation of a bromonium ion, where a curved arrow originates from the alkene double bond and points to a bromine atom in the diatomic bromine molecule, while a second arrow indicates the breakage of the Br-Br bond. This results in a three-membered cyclic bromonium intermediate. Next, the nitrogen atom of the amine group acts as an internal nucleophile; an arrow goes from the nitrogen lone pair to a carbon atom of the bromonium ring to create a six-membered ring, while another arrow points from the C-Br bond to the bromine atom to open the three-membered ring. This yields a positively charged ammonium intermediate. In the final step, a bromide ion deprotonates the nitrogen, indicated by an arrow moving from the bromide ion to the hydrogen atom attached to the nitrogen, followed by an arrow going from the N-H bond to the nitrogen atom to neutralize the charge, resulting in the final piperidine product. The second reaction is the addition of HBr to an alkene involving a carbocation rearrangement. The process begins with the protonation of the alkene, where an arrow originates from the double bond and points to the hydrogen atom of the HBr molecule, and another arrow points from the H-Br bond to the bromine atom to release a bromide ion. This generates an initial secondary carbocation. A rearrangement then occurs via a 1,2-hydride shift, indicated by a small arrow starting at the adjacent benzylic C-H bond and pointing to the positively charged carbon. This shift creates a more stable benzylic carbocation, which is stabilized by resonance. Finally, the reaction concludes with the nucleophilic attack of the bromide ion, where an arrow extends from the bromide lone pair to the positively charged benzylic carbon, forming the final rearranged alkyl bromide product.

question_text: Provide a detailed mechanism for the following transformation. Propose all the reagents used. Ignore stereochemistry. Reactant is CCCC1=CCCC1. Product is CCCC1CCCC1Br
canonical_answer: Reagents are HBr and ROOR.  The mechanism is divided into Initiation and propagation sections. In the initiation a peroxide molecule ROOR undergoes thermal homolytic cleavage to yield two alkoxy radicals. In the second step, an alkoxy radical reacts with hydrogen bromide where a single-headed curved arrow extends from the oxygen radical electron and meets a corresponding arrow from the HBr bond to form an alcohol, while a third single-headed arrow points from the H-Br bond to the bromine atom to generate a bromine radical. In the propagation phase, the bromine radical adds to 1-propylcyclopentene where a single-headed arrow originates from the double bond and meets a single-headed arrow from the bromine radical to form a CBr bond at the secondary carbon, while another single-headed arrow moves from the double bond to the tertiary carbon creating a tertiary radical intermediate. In the second step, this intermediate abstracts a hydrogen from HBr shown by a single-headed arrow from the carbon radical electron meeting a single-headed arrow from the HBr bond to form a CH bond, and a final single-headed arrow points from the HBr bond to the bromine atom to yield the final alkyl bromide product and regenerate a bromine radical.

question_text: Triphenylphosphine can be used in a two-step process to convert E alkenes to Z alkenes or vice-versa. In this example, the E starting material is first converted to the epoxide, which is then treated with Ph3P. Propose a likely mechanism, using stereochemically correct structures, for the phosphine-mediated part of the process. In the {box_1}, provide the structure of a reagent for converting the alkene to the epoxide.
canonical_answer: {box_1}:= O=C(OO)c1cccc(Cl)c1. (E)-1-phenylprop-1-ene undergoes epoxidation with meta-chloroperoxybenzoic acid to yield trans-2-methyl-3-phenyloxirane. Triphenylphosphine nucleophilically attacks the epoxide carbon, triggering C-O bond cleavage and generating a zwitterionic betaine intermediate containing a phosphonium cation and an alkoxide anion. Following rotation around the central carbon-carbon bond, the alkoxide attacks the phosphorus center to form a four-membered oxaphosphetane ring. A subsequent concerted syn-cycloreversion eliminates triphenylphosphine oxide and produces (Z)-1-phenylprop-1-ene.

question_text: Provide a curved-arrow mechanism for each of the following reactions: (a) C=C(C)C + CO, then H2SO4, then H2O = CC(C)C(=O)O; (b) CC1(C)C(=O)OC(CI)C1c2ccccc2 + Mg/THF; then [H3O+] = C=CC(c1ccccc1)C(C)(C)C(=O)O.
canonical_answer: (a) The acid-catalyzed carbonylation of 2-methylpropene initiates with the protonation of the alkene double bond, where the π-electrons attack a proton to generate a tertiary tert-butyl carbocation. Subsequently, the lone pair on the carbon atom of carbon monoxide performs a nucleophilic attack on the carbocation center, forming a resonance-stabilized acylium ion intermediate. A water molecule then acts as a nucleophile, attacking the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of the acylium species and shifting the π-electron density onto the oxygen atom. The mechanism concludes with the deprotonation of the resulting protonated carboxylic acid intermediate by a second water molecule to yield pivalic acid. (b) The transformation begins with the oxidative insertion of magnesium metal into the carbon-iodine bond of the iodomethyl-substituted lactone to generate a primary alkylmagnesium iodide intermediate. A fragmentation sequence is then triggered by the electron pair from the carbon-magnesium bond, which moves to establish a new carbon-carbon double bond. This electronic shift drives the concomitant cleavage of the adjacent lactone carbon-oxygen single bond, resulting in ring opening and the formation of a carboxylate anion paired with the magnesium halide cation. The process is completed by an acidic workup, where the carboxylate anion abstracts a proton to furnish the final unsaturated carboxylic acid product.

question_text: The antidepressant drug tranylcypromine is a primary amine with the amino group on a cyclopropane ring. Propose how you would convert an enantiomer of 2-phenylcyclopropanecarboxylic acid (which is made from trans-cinnamic acid) to tranylcypromine. (Hint: If someone is asking you to rearrange your atoms, it is best to be courteous.). What reaction would you use to effect the first transformation shown below? Trans-cinnamic acid is O=C(O)/C=C/c1ccccc1, 2-phenylcyclopropanecarboxylic acid is [H][C@]1(C(=O)O)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2, tranylcypromine is [H][C@]1(N)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2.
canonical_answer: Simmons-Smith reaction. [H][C@]1(C(=O)O)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2 + SOCl2 = [H][C@]1(C(=O)Cl)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2, [H][C@]1(C(=O)Cl)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2 + NaN3 = [H][C@]1(C(=O)N=N#N)C[C@]1([H])c2ccccc2, [H][C@]1(C(=O)N=N#N)C[C@]1([H])c2ccccc2 + heat, then H2O = [H][C@]1(N)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2; [H][C@]1(C(=O)O)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2 + SOCl2 = [H][C@]1(C(=O)Cl)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2,  [H][C@]1(C(=O)Cl)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2 + NH3, then Br2 + NaOH + H2O = [H][C@]1(N)C[C@@H]1c2ccccc2.

question_text: Alizarin yellow R is an azo dye that changes color from yellow to red between pH 10.2 and 12.2. Provide a synthesis of alizarin yellow R from aniline, salicylic acid (o-hydroxybenzoic acid), and any other reagents. Aniline is Nc1ccccc1, salycilic acid is O=C(O)c1ccccc1O, alizarin yellow R is O=C(O)c2cc(N=Nc1ccc(N(=O)=O)cc1)ccc2O.

question_text: The Wohl degradation, written below, can be used to convert an aldose into another aldose with one fewer carbon. Provide the structure of the missing compounds as well as the curved-arrow mechanisms for the conversion of {box_2} to C and C to arabinose. [H][C@](O)(C=O)[C@@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)CO + NH2OH = {box_1} (compound A), {box_1} + Ac2O (excess) = {box_2} (compound B), {box_2} + NaOAc = [H][C@@](C#N)(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(COC(C)=O)OC(C)=O (compound C), [H][C@@](C#N)(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(COC(C)=O)OC(C)=O + NaOCH3 + CH3OH = [H][C@@](O)(CO)[C@@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)C(C)=O (arabinose).
canonical_answer: {box_1}:= [H][C@](O)(/C=N\O)[C@@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)[C@]([H])(O)CO, {box_2}:= [H][C@@](/C=N\OC(C)=O)(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@]([H])(OC(C)=O)[C@@]([H])(COC(C)=O)OC(C)=O. Mechanism for conversion of B to C: the transformation of the peracetylated oxime into the nitrile is initiated by the acetate anion, which acts as a base to abstract the proton from the imine carbon. The electron pair from the broken carbon-hydrogen bond moves to establish a carbon-nitrogen triple bond, facilitating the simultaneous heterolytic cleavage of the nitrogen-oxygen bond and the expulsion of the acetoxy leaving group. Mechanism for conversion of C to arabinose: the degradation process begins with the nucleophilic attack of a methoxide anion on the carbonyl carbon of the C2-acetoxy group. This addition forms a tetrahedral intermediate which collapses to expel methyl acetate, generating a C2-alkoxide anion. The negative charge on the oxygen atom then drives the formation of a new carbonyl double bond, triggering the cleavage of the C1–C2 sigma bond and the expulsion of the cyanide anion to yield the chain-shortened aldehyde.
